data.title: Toncoin Walks A Tightrope At $2.80 As Market Tension Builds – What Next?
data.description:
In a recent post on X, Michael Steinbach highlighted that Toncoin’s current price is at $2.80, which he considers one of the most exciting levels of the year. With momentum building, Steinbach noted that traders everywhere are now asking the same question: Is a breakout finally underway, or is a sharp sell-off just around the corner?

Toncoin Locked In A Narrow Range Between $2.70 And $2.80

Analyzing the daily chart, Michael Steinbach points out that Toncoin has been locked in a tight range between $2.70 as support and $2.80 as resistance for several weeks now. He warns that jumping into the market without a clear plan is a recipe for losses, especially when others are already navigating these well-defined zones with precision.

He highlights the RSI sitting at 39, a relatively weak position. While it’s not yet in oversold territory, Steinbach notes that buyers may be holding off for deeper levels. Back in April, a strong rebound occurred from below 30, making the 30–32 zone a critical area to watch for potential bullish reactions.

In terms of risk, Steinbach warns that a break below the $2.70 support could hand control over to the bears. If that level fails, the next downside targets to watch are $2.50 and, in a worst-case scenario, $2.00. He reminds traders that repeated tests of a support zone tend to weaken it over time, and when it finally cracks, the fallout can come fast.

Whether watching for a breakout or a breakdown, having a plan is essential. Reacting after the crowd moves rarely pays off; it’s the calm, pre-planned decisions that give traders the edge when volatility strikes.

Breakout Or Pullback? Define The Setup Before Entering

In outlining the bullish scenario, the analyst noted that if Toncoin manages to secure a daily close above the $2.80 resistance, momentum could quickly follow through. This breakout could open the path toward $3.00, with an extended target near $3.40, representing a potential 26% gain from current levels. That’s the kind of upside savvy traders prepare for.

So, what’s the key takeaway? According to the analyst, successful trading doesn’t rely on gut feeling; it requires well-defined triggers. That means either entering on a confirmed breakout above $2.80 with a stop-loss just below, or stepping back and waiting for a pullback that aligns with RSI signals. The focus should always be on minimizing risk while allowing profits room to grow.

As for now, the analyst sees the trend as sideways to slightly bearish. Until the chart sends a crystal-clear signal, the best approach is patience — no FOMO trades, no blind bets, just disciplined setups.

data.title: Tesla's UK sales rise 12% in June from a year ago, New AutoMotive data shows
data.description:
Tesla's new car sales in Britain grew 12% year-on-year in June, data from research group New AutoMotive showed on Friday, as the U.S. automaker started deliveries of its updated Model Y last month.

Overall, Britain's new car registrations in June grew 12.8% from the prior year to 187,655 units, led by a 45.5% growth in battery electric vehicle sales, the data showed.

Tesla sold 7,891 units in June compared to 7,019 units in the prior year, according to New AutoMotive.

Despite the rise in sales for June, Tesla's UK sales are still down nearly 2% so far this year, while Chinese rival BYD's sales have increased nearly four-fold.

BYD's June sales in the UK also rose nearly four-fold to 2,498 units.

US automaker Ford's EV sales grew the fastest among its rivals in Britain during the first half of 2025, rising more than four-fold from the same period last year, according to the data.

data.title: Digital lender Zopa expands to Manchester in British banking push
data.description:
Digital bank Zopa is expanding to the northern English city of Manchester, where it could eventually house up to 500 staff, it said on Friday, as it continues its push to take on incumbent traditional banks such as HSBC and Barclays .

Across Europe, competitive pressure has increased in banking and a series of takeovers of mid-sized banks has taken place as players fight for scale. In the latest deal, Spanish lender Santander has said it is set to buy Britain's TSB.

Zopa, which began as a peer-to-peer lender before shifting focus to banking in 2021, last month launched a suite of bank account products. They include a savings account that pays a market-beating 7.1%, as it tries to grab market share from the traditional players that dominate the market.

Zopa will launch its Manchester office in August with initial capacity for 50 staff, it said, as it seeks to increase its current 1.5 million customers and 5.5 billion pounds in deposits.

data.title: Britain launches strategy to expand onshore wind, create jobs
data.description:
Britain is launching its first ever onshore wind strategy, designed to boost renewable power capacity and increase jobs in the sector, the government said on Friday.

Britain is aiming to largely decarbonise its electricity sector by 2030 as part of efforts to meet its climate goals, boost energy security and reduce power costs by curbing its reliance on expensive fossil fuels.

“Rolling out more onshore wind is a no-brainer – it’s one of our cheapest technologies, quick to build, supports thousands of skilled jobs and can provide clean energy directly to the communities hosting it,” Energy Minister Michael Shanks said in a statement from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.

The government said the move could help create around 45,000 jobs.

Under the strategy it is launching 40 actions to help boost onshore wind projects such as offering funding for things like football pitches or libraries in communities hosting the projects, working to repower existing wind turbines coming to the end of their lifespans and improving the planning process for new projects.

“The measures outlined will increase confidence among investors and developers, so that we can attract billions in private investment and create thousands of highly-skilled jobs and new supply chains all over the country,” James Robottom, Head of Onshore Wind Delivery at industry group RenewableUK said in the government statement.

Britain currently has around 16 gigawatts (GW) of onshore wind power but is targeting 27-29 GW by 2030.

data.title: REG-Notification for the purposes of article 13 of the Luxembourg law of 11 January 2008 on transparency requirements for issuers
data.description: Luxembourg (July 3, 2025) – Alvotech S.A. announces that on June 25, 2025, the proportion of own shares held by Alvotech S.A. reached 6.73%, crossing above the 5% threshold of the total number of voting rights. The above percentage has been calculated on the basis of 22,484,087 own shares out of 334,038,147 shares in issue to which voting rights are attached.

data.title: Beach Energy Price Target Raised 8% to A$1.35/Share by Macquarie
Australia Shares Shaping for Opening Rise — Market Talk
data.description: Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is shaping for an opening rise after U.S. stocks hit fresh highs following stronger-than-expected jobs data. ASX futures are up by 0.3% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the benchmark index also has a shot at a fresh record. The ASX 200 set its highest close Wednesday before edging less than 0.1% lower on Thursday. Ahead of the open, Magellan reported flat institutional outflows for June, with total assets under management rising by A$300 million. In the U.S., the S&P 500 climbed 0.8% and the Nasdaq Composite added 1.0% as both closed at records. The Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 0.8%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Paladin Gets New Bull as Production Recovers Rapidly — Market Talk
data.description: Paladin Energy gets a new bull in Jefferies as production from the Langer Heinrich uranium mine in Namibia recovers rapidly. Lower-than-expected feed grades from stockpiled ore and water supply issues led Paladin to cut FY 2025 guidance to 3.0 million-3.6 million lb in late 2024. The operation was then hit by wet weather. Now, Jefferies thinks Paladin could produce 975,000 lb of U3O8, a common compound of uranium, in 4Q. That is 27% above consensus hopes. "Following a period of sector underperformance, we see improved production recovery and ramp-up could lift consensus earnings outlook, and see a mean reversion in sector share price performance," analyst Daniel Roden says. Jefferies upgrades Paladin to "buy," from "hold." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1856 ET - Paladin Energy gets a new bull in Jefferies as production from the Langer Heinrich uranium mine in Namibia recovers rapidly. Lower-than-expected feed grades from stockpiled ore and water supply issues led Paladin to cut FY 2025 guidance to 3.0 million-3.6 million lb in late 2024. The operation was then hit by wet weather. Now, Jefferies thinks Paladin could produce 975,000 lb of U3O8, a common compound of uranium, in 4Q. That is 27% above consensus hopes. "Following a period of sector underperformance, we see improved production recovery and ramp-up could lift consensus earnings outlook, and see a mean reversion in sector share price performance," analyst Daniel Roden says. Jefferies upgrades Paladin to "buy," from "hold." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1811 ET - In late 2012 Jones Soda lost its Nasdaq listing, but not its enthusiasm for creative beverages. Now on the OTCQB and the Canadian Securities Exchange, it filed for a stock offering and plans to return to Nasdaq. Even its proposed stock symbol hints at its non-soda evolution, as it looks to switch to JBEV from JSDA. The filing discusses growth plans for the modern soda category including Pop Jones and Fiesta Jones, and alternative adult beverages Mary Jones and Spiked Jones, which contains alcohol. The company recently sold its cannabis beverage business, but retains hemp-derived THC products that include Mary Jones sodas, shooters and gummies. Even as it pursued edgier drinks, Jones hasn't forgotten its family-friendly offerings. Even as it pursued edgier drinks, Jones hasn't forgotten its family-friendly offerings, such as last year's limited-edition S'mores-flavored soda that combined, "the taste of melted chocolate, gooey marshmallows and crisp graham crackers." (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1749 ET - On Holding sees an opportunity to enhance its image as a premium sportswear brand with a push into higher-priced footwear and apparel, a space largely unoccupied by other players in the industry, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note following a meeting with management. Price points of about $400 and up could give a boost to On's direct-to-consumer channel as well as long-term sales and gross margins growth, the analysts say. Such a strategy looks underappreciated by investors, and may provide a tailwind in the medium term, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1730 ET - On Holding's markets in Asia and China will become increasingly key as the footwear and apparel brand targets further growth, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note after meeting with management. The company is focused on improving distribution in the region with new and existing partners, as well as opening owned and franchised stores, the analysts say. The brand should be a good fit for Asian consumers given a shift from luxury to premium, niche brands, but investors may be less willing to support On's initiatives in the region if there's a big slowdown in its markets in the Americas or Europe, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1649 ET - Like Nike, On Holding is looking to team sports as key to its medium- to long-term growth goals, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. Such a strategy is critical for expanding On's customer base, but Nike's resurgence and intense competition within sportswear could make its sports debut challenging and expensive, the analysts say. Management did not say which sports they plan to target, but soccer could be a good bet while golf and skiing could be too small, the analysts say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1641 ET - KKR is looking stronger as market volatility subsides and the threat of tariffs seems to have de-escalated, Morgan Stanley analysts Michael Cyprys and Stephanie Ma say, elevating the stock to one of its top picks. Market conditions look better for dealmaking with stronger stock currencies and improved confidence among chief executives, Cyprys and Ma say. That puts the once highly anticipated capital market rebound back on track, which in turn boosts private markets and supports a robust earnings outlook for KKR. "KKR's diversity across end markets is best positioned to benefit from secular tailwinds," they say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1622 ET - Wireless companies are cheering the passage of Republicans' massive spending bill, which offers them new tax breaks and fresh chances to bid on federal spectrum auctions. The bill orders the U.S. to privatize an estimated $85 billion worth of wireless rights, opening them up for future sale to cellphone carriers like AT&T and T-Mobile. Republicans reached a compromise on the auction authority, which lapsed in 2023, after adding safeguards for some airwaves used by military and intelligence agencies. (andrew.fitzgerald@wsj.com; @drewfitzgerald)

1612 ET - Kudos start to pour in from Corporate America following Congressional approval of President Trump's Big Beautiful Bill. AT&T says it's going to ramp up spending in its fiber infrastructure with an added 1 million customer locations annually starting in 2026, largely due to pro-investment policies laid out in the legislation. The company thanked Trump for making spectrum availability a priority, days after the president called out the carrier for issues he had during a conference call with supporters. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1448 ET - The Trump administration's budget reconciliation bill aims to boost homebuilding, Benchmark analyst Reuben Garner says. The bill, which passed through the Senate and House, proposes a new, permanent 12% housing credit allocation increase, Garner says. It also aims to cut the bond test threshold in half to 25%, which is estimated to nearly double the projected number of affordable homes built in the next decade to around 1 million, Garner says. Overall housing supply remains well below pre-pandemic levels and there is a shortage of about 5.8 million houses nationwide, Garner says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1426 ET - Heading into F1Q results next week, Aritzia looks to have a strong setup even as tariffs remain a risk, UBS analysts say in a note. The company's sales momentum likely continued in the period given strong Google Search traffic trends, up nearly 32% in the U.S. and about 17% in Canada on a two-year basis, and similarly healthy industry sales data, the analysts say. Sentiment has room to run higher as Aritzia proves it can successfully execute its growth plan in the U.S. and since the stock is less crowded than other growing brands like On Holding and Amer Sports, they say. Such factors should help shares outperform in the next year, they say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1406 ET - Brazil's economic indicators have been mostly benign, while government spending remains a source of concern, UBS analysts write. They expect the central bank to be able to start trimming its steep, 15% benchmark interest rate later this year or in early 2026. UBS labels Brazil's floating-rate and inflation-indexed bonds attractive, while fixed-rate bonds are deemed less attractive, for local portfolios. UBS keeps a neutral view on Brazilian equities, but says flows from foreign investors have been robust and valuations are appealing. The prospect of falling borrowing costs in the near future supports "a constructive view" for stocks, the analysts say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1327 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by seven this week to 425 and was down by 54 from a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. Natural gas rigs slipped by one to 108, or seven more than a year ago. In the Dallas Fed's second-quarter survey of energy companies in Texas, northern Louisiana and southern New Mexico, almost half of the executives said they expect to drill fewer wells this year than they had planned at the start of 2025. If oil were to hold at $60 a barrel, 61% of companies said they would expect a small decrease in their firms' oil production over the next year. At $50 a barrel, 46% of firms would expect a significant drop. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: US fiscal folly could create big, beautiful debt spiral: Klement
data.description:
The U.S. tax and spending bill passed on July 3 is expected to add more than $3 trillion to the country’s deficit over the next decade. If the current debt trajectory continues unabated, it could set off a slow motion debt spiral that could endanger the Federal Reserve’s independence.

The sobering long-term debt projections of the Congressional Budget Office may actually understate the likely impact on U.S. debt-to-GDP levels of President Donald Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill".

The CBO based its estimate on the assumption that temporary increases in government spending and tax cuts will sunset at a projected date. But this new budget bill, which extended previous tax cuts and other measures, has shown that this sunset often never arrives.

Thus, the long-term projections in the U.S. Treasury’s annual financial report may be more realistic since they assume the current rate of government spending will continue indefinitely. In the Treasury forecast, the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio is projected to increase to over 200% in 2050 compared to the CBO’s estimate of around 145%.

Scarier still, the Treasury forecasts that the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio will reach 535% by 2100 if current spending plans continue.

Proponents of tax cuts argue that they boost GDP growth and thus will slow the rise in debt-to-GDP, but the CBO estimates that the House Bill will only increase real GDP by an average of 0.5% over 10 years or 0.04% per year relative to the CBO’s January 2025 projections. The Tax Foundation estimates that the Senate Bill will boost GDP growth by 1.2% in the “long run”.

That hardly makes a difference compared to an expected debt increase totalling almost 10% of GDP.

RISING RISK PREMIUMS

If today’s debt dynamics persist, the risk premiums in the U.S. Treasury market will almost certainly climb over the long run.

Economists Martin Ademmer and Jamie Rush have analysed the drivers of 10-year Treasury real yields since 1970. They concluded that investors typically demand more risk compensation as the U.S. deficit increases, especially when there is competition from an ample supply of safe assets globally. Thus, Treasury yields rise.

Their analysis concludes that these two factors together lifted the natural 10-year real yield for Treasuries by 1.3 percentage points between 2005 and 2023. If the deficit projections for the next decade are realized, this trend should continue.

With all this in mind, it was notable that U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said last week that he would not boost long-term Treasury bond sales given today’s high interest rates. Since the pandemic, the average duration of U.S. government debt has declined significantly as the Treasury has favoured bills over longer-term instruments in an effort to keep interest expenses under control.

One reading of Bessent’s comments is that the Treasury is concerned about the country’s ability to continue servicing its long-term debts if it borrows at today’s elevated yields, a message that could push Treasuries’ risk premium even higher, making long-term borrowing even less tenable.

GREEK TRAGEDY

This reminds me of a similar episode in which a heavily indebted country faced a sudden spike in its already large deficit. As investors lost trust in the country’s ability to pay back its debt, long-term yields rose, which in turn forced the government to issue debt at shorter and shorter maturities. This signalled to the market that the government would struggle to pay the existing debt, and this pushed long-term government bond yields even higher. The country’s debt entered a doom loop.

The country in question: Greece after the 2009 financial crisis.

To be clear, I do not expect the U.S. to experience a similar implosion. There are crucial differences between the U.S. and Greece that should prevent this, not least the ability of the U.S. to devalue the dollar and inflate away some of its debt. Greece, as a euro zone member, had no such flexibility.

But the new U.S. budget increases the possibility that the U.S. could face a similar debt drama, only in slow motion. If long-term Treasury yields remain higher for longer, the Treasury is apt to continue shortening the duration of its debt. This, in turn, could create a vicious cycle by making government interest expenses more volatile, further imperiling U.S. fiscal health and making longer-term debt even riskier.

OFF RAMPS

There appear to be three main off ramps for the U.S.

One: politicians could become fiscally prudent and significantly reduce the deficit to a sustainable level. This seems unlikely given both parties’ recent track records.

Two: the Treasury could impose capital controls to artificially increase demand for Treasuries. As I have written previously, this move would likely spell the end of the dollar as the main global reserve currency.

Three: the Fed could create artificial demand for long-term Treasuries by scooping up bonds itself – that is, restarting quantitative easing – to keep yields low. The danger with this form of QE, however, is that it represents fiscal dominance, where the central bank loses control over monetary policy because of imprudent government actions.

How such a development would play out is impossible to predict, especially when it involves a global superpower, but it’s fair to assume the Fed won’t want to find out.

(The views expressed here are those of Joachim Klement, an investment strategist at Panmure Liberum, the UK's largest independent investment bank).

Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, and X.

data.title: Value Emerging in Diversified Miners Despite Headwinds — Market Talk
data.description: Value is starting to emerge in ASX-listed diversified miners, says UBS. Still, iron ore and copper prices are a near-term worry. UBS raises estimates for the iron ore price in 2025 and 2026 by 4% and 6% to US$97/ton and US$90/ton, respectively. But it expects the start up of the new Simandou iron-ore mine in Guinea to put pressure on prices, driving them some US$10/ton lower in 2026. "On copper, we are cautious near-term on potential U.S. S232 investigation of copper import tariffs to weigh on LME pricing in the short term," analyst Lachlan Shaw says. It prefers BHP to Rio Tinto, given the latter's exposure to Mongolia and Guinea and geopolitical risk in both of those countries. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Uranium Market About to Enter Lull — Market Talk
data.description: The global uranium market is about to enter a lull, says Jefferies. Uranium demand remains structurally strong, and sentiment in the sector continues to improve, analyst Daniel Roden says. Still, Jefferies expects the market to seasonally soften over the next few months as utilities in the Northern Hemisphere buy less over their summer. It forecasts a 3Q uranium price of US$72.5/lb. "Transaction volumes typically decline in this period," Jefferies says. It expects a more robust market from September. "The persistence of firm term prices throughout the current dip reinforces the tightness in forward supply and underpins our constructive outlook for uranium into late 2025," says Jefferies. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Gold Reserve Emerges as Top Bidder for Venezuela's Citgo — WSJ
data.description:
By Andrew Scurria

A court-appointed special master recommended a nearly $7.4 billion bid from Venezuela creditor Gold Reserve for the country's state-owned oil refiner Citgo Petroleum, roughly double the prior leading offer.

Robert Pincus, the special master overseeing the forced sale of Venezuela's stake in Citgo, said in court papers filed Wednesday that the bid from Gold Reserve's Dalinar Energy is the highest and best offer. Citgo, among the largest U.S.-based oil refiners, is being auctioned to cover debts owed to bondholders and multinational corporations by its owner, the bankrupt Venezuelan government.

Dalinar is backed by Gold Reserve, which holds a $1.1 billion judgment, as well as two subsidiaries of Koch, also a judgment creditor of Venezuela. Dalinar emerged as the leading bidder, or stalking horse, after the special master previously designated offers from Elliott Investment Management and Contrarian Capital Management as lead bids.

Bidding got off to a slow start after a federal judge ordered Venezuela's shares in Citgo to be sold, in part because the sale didn't include the kind of buyer protections typically present in a bankruptcy sale. Bidders contended with the risk that U.S. courts could hold Citgo responsible for judgments held by other creditors of Venezuela, which could erode its equity value.

But that risk lessened after a federal judge in New York in February held that Citgo's U.S. holding company shouldn't be considered an extension of the Venezuelan state and liable for its obligations.

The U.S. government in 2019 placed Citgo under the control of Venezuela's U.S.-backed opposition leaders, and in 2023 cleared the way for a forced sale of the shares of its parent company, PDV Holding. Lawyers for PDVH have fought in court to block the sale at every step.

Any sale requires approval from U.S. District Judge Leonard P. Stark and could be challenged by other bidders. The judge has set a sale hearing for later this year.

One source of litigation risk comes from a group of Venezuela bondholders owed $1.7 billion with collateral rights over Citgo's operating assets. Bondholders have previously indicated they could try to interfere with a sale like Dalinar's that relies on debt financing and could push them down in the payment line, court papers show.

Write to Andrew Scurria at Andrew.Scurria@wsj.com

data.title: How Far Does $1,000 Take You on a Trip to Chicago? We Found Out. — WSJ
data.description:
By Jason Diamond | Photographs by Lyndon French for WSJ

I have lived in New York most of my life, but I was born in Chicago and I try to get back at least once a year. I was overdue, and so when The Wall Street Journal asked me to see how far I could stretch $1,000 in my hometown, I jumped at the opportunity, though I saw one wrinkle to the plan. I had a physical booked for when I returned to New York, so I introduced an additional catch. I'd eat cheaply — but also healthy. Here's how I fared.

Day One | $1,000 remaining

Upon landing, I bought a $15 3-day unlimited public transit pass. An Uber from the airport can cost $100 with surge pricing. On the L train from O'Hare, I zipped past clogged traffic on the expressway.

Hunger and nostalgia can be a dangerous combination. Surely, I told myself, any reasonable cardiologist would agree that a single Chicago-style hot dog, with its salad-bar abundance of toppings, isn't excessively unhealthy. I made my way to West Ridge in the city's northernmost reaches, for the $7.99 dog, fries and soda deal at Wolfy's, a childhood favorite.

I took the train downtown and checked into Le Méridien Essex Chicago. The $540 bill for two nights cut my budget in half, but its central location is hard to beat. After checking in, I walked through Downtown, past a procession of beautiful buildings, old and new, one after another: Louis Sullivan within walking distance of Mies van der Rohe.

Next, I went to Gus' Sip and Dip in River North. I had originally resolved to avoid alcohol on this health-conscious trip. But, I reasoned, I'd earned a little treat after a long travel day that started when my 14-month-old woke me at 5:30 a.m. I sheepishly ordered a dirty Martini. I relished the first sip and looked to my left. Someone was eating a Wagyu beef sandwich, dipping it into a cup of beef juice between bites. My mission fell apart as I ordered one of my own. The bill came out to $55 plus a nice tip for the bartender who explained how a collection of John Belushi's "Saturday Night Live" cue cards had ended up framed on the wall: The late comedian's brother, Jim Belushi, is a Gus fan.

Satiated, I joined a river cruise hosted by the Chicago Architecture Center ($70). The sun started to set just as we passed Bertrand Goldberg's Marina City. Predictably, several middle-aged dads stood up to take pictures of it; one explained to his bored teenage son, "That's the building from the Wilco album cover."

Day Two | $312 remaining

Half of my family comes from the South Side of Chicago and I've always considered it a shame that most visitors don't spend any time there. Yes, it has rough sections like any big-city district might, but it also hosts over 100 spots on the National Register of Historic Places and, as one especially carnivorous friend claimed, the nation's best brisket.

First, I stopped at 57th Street Books in Hyde Park. I spent $15 on a paperback, and then another $5 on coffee so I could have something to sip as I sat and stared at Frank Lloyd Wright's magnificent brick creation on the University of Chicago campus, the Frederick C. Robie House.

I spent $3.75 on a Metra train ticket (not included in my pass) to reach Lexington Betty Smokehouse, hidden among chains in a nondescript shopping plaza. My willpower withered, but my friend was right. I'd put Chef Dominique Leach's brisket up against anything in Texas Hill Country. After I finished, I walked the grounds of Pullman National Historic Park, George Pullman's 19th-century company town where workers lived and built train cars.

I had time to kill between my South Side jaunt and a screening of "Chinatown" up in Wrigleyville, so I took the Red Line to its northernmost terminus, then walked to the Fish Keg. I love lake fish, which my New England-born wife likes to joke about. She can't understand how fish from Lake Michigan can taste as good as anything caught in the Atlantic. The Fish Keg could convince her. It always satisfies my craving for deep-fried perch or walleye, washed down with an RC Cola ($14).

Properly fortified, I headed to the Music Box Theatre. Other cities might boast more famous repertory theaters, but my favorite remains the Music Box with its grand marquee and old organ that still accompanies silent films. As I settled into my seat to watch "Chinatown" ($14), I batted away intrusive thoughts about my derailed diet. How had I failed so miserably in my mission, nearing two full days without eating a fresh vegetable? To paraphrase the 1974 masterpiece, "Forget it, Jason. It's Chicago."

Day Three | $246 left

I started my day with breakfast at Dove's Luncheonette, where I ordered tacos norteños with mushrooms and an avocado ($39), and patted myself on the back for finally eating something that grew from the ground. At Alcala's Western Wear nearby, I bought a little Wrangler dress with cowboys on it for my daughter and a ring for my wife ($44 total). After that, I wandered to Avondale Bowl on the outskirts of Logan Square. Originally built in the 1930s, the small, eight-lane, all-wood alley had by the end of the 20th century fallen into disrepair. A recent rehab that seamlessly combines art deco and Midcentury Modern design elements, brought it back to life. An hour of solo bowling only ran me $20, so I treated myself to a cocktail as I had time and cash to spare. The only thing left on my agenda was to see the White Sox play.

Sadly, nature had other plans. I had already crisscrossed the city multiple times and rain made me reconsider schlepping 45 more minutes by train to Rate Field on the South Side. Plus, I needed a decent salad at all costs, which I found at the Drawing Room, a restaurant at the Chicago Athletic Association, a 19th-century men's club that's been converted into a hotel. The beet salad with a little wine came to $40. I might have never quite figured out how to consistently eat healthy in this city, but maybe that's OK. Nowhere does big, beefy, or crispy better than Chicago.

What to SKIP and Where to SPEND

Three strategies for making your bucks go far in Chicago

SKIP cheap hotels, SPEND on a good location

Be ready to drop at least $130 or so a night on a decent hotel. Options abound downtown so there's no excuse to end up far from the action.

SKIP the guidebook lists, SPEND on local classics

Get breakfast at one of the city's old-school cafeterias like Valois on the South Side or Manny's Deli on the West Side. Both draw local politicians, a reliable marker of authenticity. For other meals, I follow a simple rule: If a lot of autographed pictures are on the walls, I'm in good hands.

SKIP the Cubs, SPEND on the White Sox

Wrigley Field, home of the Cubs, has become a major tourist destination, but, across town, the White Sox perform so poorly that decent tickets can be found for around $10. Rate Field only consistently draws die-hards, which means you'll have room to kick back and relax during the game. Wrigley, by contrast, feels like a big, drunken party when the Cubs play.

The Wall Street Journal is not compensated by retailers listed in its articles as outlets for products. Listed retailers frequently are not the sole retail outlets.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 7 PM ET: U.S. Stocks Reach Another Closing High After Jobs Report Stronger Than Expected | Steady ...
data.description:
U.S. Stocks Reach Another Closing High After Jobs Report Stronger Than Expected

The S&P 500 rose to a record for the seventh time this year, while the Nasdaq also ended the shortened week at an all-time high.

----

Steady Hiring Added 147,000 Jobs to U.S. Economy in June

The jobs report showed manufacturers are pausing hiring amid tariff uncertainty, while unemployment fell partly because fewer people are looking for work.

----

June Jobs Report Likely to Extend Fed's Summer Pause

Fed officials have cited solid labor-market readings to justify a wait-and-see stance.

----

House Passes Trump's Megabill in GOP Triumph

The narrow vote delivered the president a major second-term legislative victory.

----

Biggest part of the U.S. economy rebounds from trade-war blues. But it's still weak.

The large service side of the economy rebounded in June after a brief slump in the prior month when U.S. trade wars were at their zenith and companies were paralyzed by indecision.

----

Canada Goods Trade Deficit Narrows as Flows With U.S. Continue to Slacken

Helped by volatile gold shipments, the May deficit was down from a record in April and continued to reflect fluctuating tariff policy in Canada's biggest trade partner and neighbor.

----

Summer Road Trips to Drive Record Ethanol Use

A record number of drivers are hitting the road this summer, giving the U.S. ethanol industry a needed shot in the arm.

----

Trump Says He Made No Progress With Putin on Ukraine in Call

He urged Russia's leader to quickly end the war in Ukraine, according to a Kremlin briefing on the hourlong call.

----

Vietnam Trade Deal Takes Aim at Back Door for Chinese Goods

The deal with Vietnam seeks to stop Chinese-made goods from transiting through the Southeast Asian country to avoid higher duties.

----

U.K. Government Bonds, Pound Recover After Prime Minister Backs Treasury Chief

Keir Starmer initially refused to confirm if Rachel Reeves would remain in post, causing sterling and gilts to sell off sharply.

----

China's Service Sector Activity Expands at Softer Pace

A private gauge of China's services sector activity expanded in June amid efforts by businesses to attract new customers.

----

A Few Members Saw a Case for Unchanged Key Rate in June, ECB Says

The fact they already considered keeping rates on hold will likely underpin expectations that the central bank will leave its key rate unchanged later this month.

----

BOJ Should Stand Ready to Hike Rates, Board Member Takata Says

The Bank of Japan should be ready to resume policy tightening if U.S. trade talks progress, policy board member Hajime Takata said.

----

Private-Equity Fundraising Sputters Through 2025's First Half

U.S. buyout and growth firms are on track for their third consecutive year of fundraising declines.

----

Tesla Stock Diehards Don't Give an Inch

Challenges are piling up for the electric-vehicle maker and CEO Elon Musk, but the company's legion of individual investors are staying put.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 7 PM ET: UPS Offers Buyouts to Drivers, a First in Its 117-Year History | JPMorgan ...
data.description:
UPS Offers Buyouts to Drivers, a First in Its 117-Year History

The company is cutting costs because of flat parcel volumes, rising labor costs and a long stock-price slide.

----

JPMorgan Is Revamping Its Bank for the Superrich to Cater to Global Clientele

David Frame is named global head of the private bank, helping deep-pocketed clients invest more abroad.

----

Starboard Takes 9% Stake in Tripadvisor

The travel-review company's shares have fallen after it rejected takeover offers last year.

----

The Score: Nike, Paramount, Constellation and More Stocks That Defined the Week

Here are some of the major companies whose stocks moved on the week's news.

----

The Corporate Winners and Losers in Trump's Big Tax Bill

Oil drillers win access to public lands and manufacturers get tax breaks, while elite universities face new endowment taxes and solar projects lose tax breaks.

----

Apollo-Backed Athora Buys U.K. Insurer for $7.8 Billion

The acquisition of Pension Insurance Corp. would effectively turn the specialist insurer into Athora's U.K. insurance business.

----

Meta Offers to Buy Stake in Venture Funds Started by AI Hires Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross

Limited partners could cash out of a minority of their holdings in venture firm NFDG at current values in a secondary deal with Meta.

----

Thoma Bravo to Take Olo Private in $2 Billion All-Cash Deal

Olo shareholders will receive $10.25 a share, a 65% premium over the share price prior to deal speculation. Shares jumped on the announcement.

----

Krispy Kreme Finance Chief Jeremiah Ashukian Leaving

Krispy Kreme's top finance executive, Jeremiah Ashukian, is leaving the doughnut maker to pursue an opportunity with a private company.

----

Airbus, ASML, Mistral Ask EU to Pause AI Rules

CEOs said the bloc's ability to become a leader in nascent AI is being disrupted by overlapping and complicated regulations.

----

Tesla Is in Disarray. Musk Has Already Moved Beyond Caring About Cars.

The electric-vehicle maker has an aging lineup and slumping sales, but its CEO has shifted his focus to robotaxis and robots.

----

Gold Reserve Emerges as Top Bidder for Venezuela's Citgo

A court-appointed special master recommended a bid backed by Gold Reserve and Koch over a rival hedge-fund offer.

----

U.S. Eases Some Chip Software Curbs on China

The U.S. has lifted some curbs on exports of chip-design software to China, according to three companies that say they are working to restore access to recently restricted products.

----

Advanced Micro Devices CEO to Get Raise, $33 Million Equity Award

All of the five AMD executives included in the securities filing are getting a raise between 3% and 5%.

----

Tech Earnings Are Around the Corner. Keep an Eye Out for These Themes.

All of the Magnificent Seven companies, except for Nvidia, are expected to report their next set of financial results at the end July.

data.title: Breaking Barriers: XRP Mining’s Innovative App Opens Global Access to Secure Cloud Mining
data.description:
With a focus on simplicity and security, XRP Mining’s new app empowers users worldwide to participate in crypto mining without technical complexity. This breakthrough solution removes traditional entry barriers, offering fast setup, stable returns, and 24/7 cloud-based performance—all from your smartphone.

Chicago, Illinois, July 03, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — With a focus on simplicity and security, XRP Mining’s new app empowers users worldwide to participate in crypto mining without technical complexity. This breakthrough solution removes traditional entry barriers, offering fast setup, stable returns, and 24/7 cloud-based performance—all from your smartphone.

XRP Mining has officially launched a new generation of cloud mining applications designed to make digital asset mining accessible to everyone - regardless of their technical expertise or financial background. This groundbreaking platform provides users around the world with a simple and easy way to mine cryptocurrencies without having to purchase expensive equipment or master complex software setups.

Traditional crypto mining can be overwhelming, requiring substantial investments in high-performance hardware, specialized knowledge, and constant system maintenance. XRP Mining has eliminated these barriers with a solution that’s both accessible and efficient. By providing rented computing power through the cloud, we allow everyday users to start mining instantly, all managed through an intuitive app interface.

Easy Process to Start Mining

Quick Registration and Immediate Activation

Sign Up: Visit xrpmining.com, register with your email, and receive your instant $15 bonus.

Select a Plan: Choose from a suite of mining contracts aligned with your budget and goals.

Fund Your Account: Deposit via a range of cryptocurrencies including Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Ripple (XRP), Litecoin (LTC), Dogecoin (DOGE), Bitcoin Cash (BCH), Solana (SOL), USDT (TRC20 & ERC20), USDC and more.

Once your account is funded and your contract selected, mining begins automatically. Daily earnings are calculated and credited the next day. When your balance reaches $100, you can either withdraw to your personal wallet or reinvest to increase your mining power.

Instant Conversion for Stability and Convenience

All contracts are denominated in USD to protect against volatility. When you deposit funds, we instantly convert your crypto to USD at the current rate. Upon withdrawal, your balance is reconverted to your preferred cryptocurrency, based on real-time rates, and sent directly to your wallet.

Key Benefits of the New XRP Mining App

Powerful, User-Friendly Dashboard

Our app combines robust functionality with simplicity. The clean dashboard gives users instant visibility over their mining contracts, daily returns, historical earnings, and current balances — all in real-time. From managing deposits to tracking profits, everything is streamlined for ease of use.

Top-Grade Security

Security is fundamental. That’s why we’ve incorporated advanced protective measures through McAfee® and Cloudflare®, defending against data breaches and cyber threats. Whether you’re accessing your account from home or traveling, your digital assets and private information are secured by the best in the industry.

Attractive Welcome Incentives

To help users get started effortlessly, XRP Mining offers:

A $15 bonus upon registration.Daily login rewards of $0.6, simply for accessing your account.These incentives encourage new miners to build momentum without upfront stress.

Broad Range of Investment Packages

We understand different users have different objectives. That’s why our cloud mining contracts start as low as $15 for a one-day plan, scaling up to longer-term options for sustained growth. Whether you want to cautiously explore or aggressively build a mining income, XRP Mining delivers packages to suit every financial goal.

24/7 Global Customer Support

Our multilingual support team is available around the clock to assist with everything from setting up accounts to clarifying payout schedules. This ensures a smooth mining experience no matter where you are or what language you speak.

Why Choose XRP Mining Over Traditional Mining?

Unlike setting up personal mining rigs that require continuous upgrades, noise control, cooling systems, and constant technical troubleshooting, XRP Mining handles all complexities on your behalf. Users simply pick a contract, monitor returns, and enjoy hassle-free earnings. This low-barrier model means no electricity concerns, no hardware risks, and no hidden operational costs.

Staying Ahead in a Competitive Market

With the global economy facing uncertainty and digital assets playing an increasingly vital role in diversified portfolios, secure cloud mining has emerged as a smart alternative. XRP Mining stands out by offering stability, flexible currency choices, rigorous security, and an environmentally conscious approach.

Get Started With XRP Mining

Whether you're a retiree looking to supplement income, a young professional diversifying investments, or simply someone curious about digital assets, XRP Mining offers a safe, transparent, and effective way to participate in the crypto revolution.

Email: info@xrpmining.com

Official Website: https://xrpmining.comApp Download: https://xrpmining.com/xml/index.html#/app

Disclaimer: The information provided in this press release does not constitute an investment solicitation, nor does it constitute investment advice, financial advice, or trading recommendations. Cryptocurrency mining and staking involve risks. There is a possibility of financial loss. It is strongly recommended that you perform due diligence before investing or trading in cryptocurrencies and securities, including consulting a professional financial advisor.

Name: Darlene Wilson

Email: info@xrpmining.com

data.title: Barclays raises Brent forecast to $72 per barrel for 2025
data.description:
Barclays on Thursday said it raised its Brent oil price forecast by $6 to $72 per barrel for 2025 and by $10 to $70 a barrel for 2026 on an improved outlook for demand.

"Geopolitical tensions have eased as the U.S​.-​mediated ceasefire between Israel and Iran continues to hold and the risk premium has evaporated, but price action has been reflecting better​-​than​-​expected fundamentals, in our view," said Barclays in a note.

Despite an accelerated increase in output from the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and its allies including Russia (OPEC+), global crude oil inventories declined in the second quarter, Barclays said.

The tighter balance outlook is driven by stronger demand growth, weaker non-OPEC supply growth, and the International Energy Agency's (IEA) upward revision of baseline demand estimates, it said.

Barclays raised its outlook for global demand growth by 260,000 barrels per day, with most of that coming from Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries, where it said "demand has been coming in stronger than expected".

It now sees U.S. oil demand growing by 130,000 bpd this year, which is 100,000 bpd ahead of its previous estimate following a weather-related demand boost earlier in the year, although it still expects a gradual slowdown in activity.

On the supply side, while OPEC+ will likely continue to phase out its voluntary production cuts at an accelerated pace, the actual output increase likely will continue to lag, Barclays said, pointing to pressure on some OPEC+ producers to curb output to compensate for earlier producing above their quotas.

"Between March and May 2025, OPEC+ target increased by 548 kb/d but the group's output remained largely flat, resulting in better compliance, in aggregate," it said.

data.title: US lets GE restart jet engine shipments to China's COMAC, source says
data.description:
The U.S. told GE Aerospace on Thursday that it can restart jet engine shipments to China's COMAC, according to a person familiar with the matter, in a further sign of de-escalating U.S.-Sino trade tensions that included concessions from Beijing over rare earths.

The United States this week also lifted restrictions on exports to China for chip design software developers and ethane producers, suggesting trade talks between the two countries are moving forward.

License suspensions and new license requirements on the different exports had been issued several weeks ago as part of the ongoing trade war between the world's two biggest economies.

GE did not respond to an email request for comment, nor did the Commerce Department, which notified GE it could restart shipments.

Licenses for GE Aerospace affect engines sold to China's state-owned aerospace manufacturer COMAC, which wants to compete internationally against dominant plane makers Airbus and Boeing .

A spokesperson for the Chinese embassy in Washington did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The restrictions were among the many countermeasures imposed by U.S. President Donald Trump's administration in response to China's export restrictions on rare earths and related magnets in April.

Beijing's move on rare earths, part of retaliation against Trump's earlier tariffs this year, has upended supply chains central to automakers, aerospace manufacturers, semiconductor companies and military contractors. The issue threatened to scupper a bilateral trade deal.

The license suspensions lifted for GE affect LEAP-1C engines to COMAC for its C919 single-aisle aircraft, and GE's CF34 engine for COMAC's C909 regional jet, according to the person familiar, who declined to be identified because they were not authorized to speak publicly.

The LEAP 1-C engines are the product of a joint venture between GE Aerospace and France's Safran .

The C919 is made in China but many of its components come from overseas.

At least one other aerospace company also had its license suspensions for China lifted on Thursday, according to another person, who declined to identify the company.

Honeywell Aerospace has supplied COMAC's C919, too, providing an auxiliary power system, wheels and brakes, flight control package, and navigation package. Honeywell did not return a request for comment.

Collins Aerospace, a subsidiary of RTX , which also supplies components for COMAC, declined to comment on the status of its licenses.

In recent weeks, the U.S. also suspended licenses for nuclear equipment suppliers to sell to China's power plants. U.S. nuclear equipment suppliers include Westinghouse and Emerson .

data.title: Australian Household Spending Numbers Key Ahead of RBA Meeting — Market Talk
data.description: Australia's household spending indicator for May at 0130 GMT will attract a lot of attention following recent retail activity indicators suggesting consumers are lacking the confidence needed to boost consumption. A weak HSI, which is a broader measure of spending than the monthly retail sales data, will further support market pricing for an interest rate cut next week. The RBA is expected to cut interest rates by further 25 basis points, but there's also an argument that policy makers may wait until August when they will have seen the latest quarterly CPI numbers. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)
